hook: You already know how to work with vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $\mathbb{R}^3$ — arrows you can add and scale. But what if the same powerful rules applied to polynomials, functions, or even grids of numbers over a clock-like number system? This unit reveals the common skeleton beneath all of these, turning scattered ideas into one elegant framework.
concept: Every topic here builds toward understanding what a 'space' of mathematical objects really is and how to measure its essential complexity — captured formally by the notion of a *vector space over a field* and its dimension.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Introduction to Fields (Real, Complex, Finite Fields)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn what a field is and recognize how $\mathbb{R}$, $\mathbb{C}$, and finite fields like $\mathbb{Z}_p$ each supply the scalars that vector spaces depend on.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Definition and Axioms of a Vector Space
topic_roadmap.description: Master the eight axioms that define a vector space so you can verify — or disprove — whether any set with addition and scalar multiplication qualifies.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Examples of Vector Spaces (Function Spaces, Polynomial Spaces)
topic_roadmap.description: Apply the vector space axioms to surprising settings like $\mathcal{C}[a,b]$ and $P_n$ so you see that 'vector' means far more than an arrow in the plane.
You will learn the three-condition subspace test and use it to identify when a subset inherits the full structure of a vector space without re-checking all axioms.
You will construct the null space and column space of a matrix and interpret them as concrete subspaces that encode a linear system's solution structure.
Master how to find a basis — a minimal spanning set of linearly independent vectors — and assign every vector space a single number, its dimension, that captures its size.
You will prove and apply the Rank-Nullity Theorem, $\text{rank}(A) + \text{nullity}(A) = n$, to instantly relate a matrix's column space and null space dimensions to the number of unknowns.
